---
title: "CISP 360 \n Structured Programming"
subtitle: "Introduction to C++"
author: "Dr. Caleb Fowler"
version: "1.0.0"
# Changelog:
# 1.0.0 (2026-03-13) - Week 9, Lecture 1: Compiler internals, types, overflow, casting

format:
  revealjs:
    margin: 0.05
    css: ../styles/styles.css
    theme: serif
    slide-number: true
    navigation-mode: vertical
    transition: slide
    incremental: false
    scrollable: false
    controls: true
    hash: true
    history: true
    title-slide: false
    footer: "CISP 360 · Fowler"
    keyboard: true
    include-after-body:
      - text: |
          <script>
          window.Reveal.on('ready', event => {
            Reveal.configure({
              keyboard: {
                34: 'down',
                33: 'up',
                116: 'right',
                190: 'left',
                79: null
              }
            });
          });
          </script>

engine: jupyter
execute:
  eval: true
  echo: true

resources:
 - topics/**/images/**

---

## Last Time

::: {.incremental}
1. **The While Loop**: An entry-condition loop that keeps running as long as its condition is true. Initialize the loop control variable before the loop, test in the condition, update inside the body.
2. **Do-While and For Loops**: Do-while is an exit-condition loop — it always runs at least once. For is a counter-controlled loop with init/condition/update in one line.
3. **Which Loop to Use**: For a known count, use for. When it must run at least once, use do-while. For everything else, use while.
:::

---

## Today's Agenda

- How the compiler works
- The symbol table
- Overflow and underflow
- Data type conversion and casting
- Signed vs unsigned variables
- Fixed-width integers and size_t
- Determining variable size

::: notes

:::

---

{{< include topics/topic_1_compiler_works.qmd >}}
{{< include topics/topic_2_symbol_table.qmd >}}
{{< include topics/topic_3_overflow_underflow.qmd >}}
{{< include topics/topic_4_whats_output_overflow.qmd >}}
{{< include topics/topic_5_type_conversion_casting.qmd >}}
{{< include topics/topic_6_whats_output_casting.qmd >}}
{{< include topics/topic_7_signed_unsigned.qmd >}}
{{< include topics/topic_8_fixed_width_size_t.qmd >}}
{{< include topics/topic_9_determining_variable_size.qmd >}}

---

# Key Takeaways

- The compiler checks types at compile time — most errors you can make with data types are caught before the program runs.
- Signed integer overflow is undefined behavior. Unsigned overflow wraps predictably.
- Use `static_cast<>` for intentional conversions. Integer division truncates — cast one operand to get a decimal result.
- Default to `int`; use `size_t` for indices and sizes; use fixed-width types when exact bit width matters.

---

# Next Time

- Strings in depth: `string` operations, input, and manipulation
